464da9fa87b033fbc74a.text:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member onboarding**: no documented first-touch-to-first-class journey (intake form → induction → coach assignment → goal-setting)
- **Churn & win-back**: no process for pause requests, cancellation reasons, or re-engagement triggers
- **Coach development**: no pathway for skill progression, feedback cycles, or quality standards enforcement
- **Class quality assurance**: no audit mechanism or consistency check across coaches
- **Member outcomes & accountability**: no system for tracking progress, milestone recognition, or retention touchpoints (30-day goals, transformations)
- **Supply chain**: no ordering/stock/inventory SOP for equipment, supplements, or merchandise
- **Data governance**: no GDPR/privacy protocol for member data handling, consent, or retention
- **Incident & complaints**: no formal escalation or resolution process for injuries, dissatisfaction, or disputes
- **Referral programme**: no structured mechanics, incentives, or tracking for member acquisition via word-of-mouth

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Marketing SOPs (5 built)**: likely missing launch sequencing, content calendar cadence, and paid campaign approval workflows
- **Coach SOPs (20 built)**: gaps around performance feedback loops, class consistency standards, and escalation paths for difficult members
- **Client SOPs (23 built)**: probably lack retention/engagement triggers and proactive outreach cadence (e.g., attendance drop alerts)
- **New quarter program launch**: missing pre-launch comms, coach briefing, and member communication timeline

---

# **Build next**

1. **Member Onboarding & First 30 Days** (highest leverage for retention)  
   *Structure*: intake questionnaire → health screening → coach pairing logic → welcome sequence → week 1 check-in → week 4 goal review → retention flag

ef78fa4eea1cbb63abd4.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO REPORT

## IMMEDIATE SITUATION
May–June shows **revenue up 12% but profit down 84%** (£5,916 → £935). Non-people expenses spiked June (+1.6% of revenue vs May's 45.7% → June's 53.0%). Active clients grew 20% May–June (122→147), but churn in June returned to zero — good retention. The bigger studio is attracting members; cost control is the emergency.

**Comparing Q2 (April–June) vs Q1 (Jan–March): Revenue +28% but Q2 profit obliterated by April fit-out. Excluding April, May–June average £18,955 revenue, £3,425 profit (18.1% margin) — well below target. The studio expansion hasn't yet delivered the profit uplift it should.**

---

## 1. INCREASE REVENUE

- **Raise headline membership price by 8–10% (£15–20/month).** June AR/M dropped to £120.48 — the lowest on record. You've grown headcount 73% since September (85→147) but unit revenue fell 26%. Larger studio justifies premium pricing; phased increase across new joiners protects retention.

- **Upsell PT/small-group classes aggressively.** At 147 members and only £120 ARPU, you're leaving money on the table. Target 20–25% uptake of add-on services (£25–40/session bundles); June non-class revenue invisible in data. Assign one staff member to retention/upsell calls.

- **Plug quiet off-peak slots with off-peak membership tier (£25/month, 9am–2pm access only).** Larger studio means more capacity; you can absorb 15–20 lower-paying members without cannibalizing prime-time revenue. June headcount grew but revenue didn't track it — pricing leakage confirmed.

---

## 2. REDUCE EXPENSES

- **Non-people expenses are the crisis: June £9,390 (53% of revenue).** May was £9,233; the delta is small but structural. Audit utilities, rent, insurance, supplies: the new studio is larger so costs *should* be higher, but 53% is unsustainable. If rent/utilities are fixed, you need £25k+ revenue/month to hit 30% margin. **Call your landlord and vendors now — locked into old lease pricing?** Renegotiate or cost-justify the new unit immediately.

- **People costs proport

f527670fd10b0672288e.text:
• **Redistribute Grace's classes immediately**: her Build: Lower (17 sessions) should shift to JoJo; Build: Upper (9) and Elevate (6) to Eilis. Strongevity (3) and No Sweat Intro (3) can rotate between JoJo and Eilis depending on who has capacity that week. This removes Grace from delivery entirely.

• **JoJo is significantly overloaded at 133 sessions**: after taking Grace's Build: Lower, she'll be at ~150. Reduce her personal training from 44 to 28–30 by moving 14–16 PT slots to Eilis (who has only 11). This balances the workload and develops Eilis's 1:1 skills.

• **Eilis is under-used and ready to grow**: she's on 48 sessions including 11 PT. Increase her PT load to 25–27, add Grace's Elevate and some of Build: Upper, and give her Strongevity and No Sweat Intro to broaden her class range. She'll sit around 95–105 sessions — a healthier load.

• **Body Composition scans (40 total last month) are inflating coaching numbers and masking real capacity**: assign these tactically by availability, not by coach. This frees up ~3–4 hours per coach per month for actual teaching or rest.

• **JoJo remains the only Tuesday coach (respected constraint)**, but her overall volume is too high to sustain. Once PT and Grace's classes are redistributed, her weekly pattern will be more sustainable; monitor her burnout risk and consider a freelance or apprentice coach for non-Tuesday capacity support within 2–3 months.

**For quiet studio gaps:**

1. Add a 45-minute "Open Gym + Coach" slot on Monday and Thursday evenings (your quietest days) — members can drop in to use equipment with a coach available for form checks or quick questions, charged at £5-8 per session to offset staffing and boost Monday/Thursday footfall.

2. Run a monthly "Barbell Basics Workshop" (Saturday morning, 30 mins) targeting newer members before a Barbelle class — teach setup, safety, and confidence-building so Barbelle feels less intimidating; position it as a free add-on or £3 upsell.

**For quieter classes:**

3. Move Engine from its current slot to a post-work Tuesday or Wednesday 6pm slot (your peak days) — it's short and intense, which suits people grabbing a quick session after work; promote it as "15-min metabolic finisher" to Build class attendees as a pairing option.

47b0548900970eee6400.text:
# Service Menu Execution — The Forge

• **Start with your revenue reality, not your wishes.** Audit last 3 months: which services actually generate money? Which do coaches actually deliver consistently? Build the menu from what *works*, not what sounds good. This stops you selling things you can't reliably deliver.

• **Separate coach-facing from member-facing ruthlessly.** Coaches need: service codes, time allocations, upsell triggers, commission structure. Members need: benefits in plain language, pricing tiers, what's included vs. add-on. Write two documents, not one hybrid that confuses both.

• **Price anchoring move:** Lock your premium tier *first* (e.g., "VIP coaching package"), then build economy options below it. Members perceive value downward. If you start cheap and work up, everything feels expensive.

• **Create a simple decision tree coaches use daily.** When a member walks in, coaches should instantly know: "New member → this package. Already trained 6 weeks → suggest this upgrade. Wants accountability → bundle that." Removes guesswork, speeds onboarding, increases attach rates.

• **Test it internally for 2 weeks before launch.** Have coaches actually *sell* from the menu to members. You'll immediately spot pricing confusion, missing options, and upsells that don't land. Fix before it goes public.

What's your current highest-revenue service right now?

e4aeda2aa0d1508a8f79.text:
**Insights**

Your cost-per-lead has collapsed to £3 (brilliant) but you're drowning in unqualified traffic—July's 3% close rate versus April's 29% tells the real story. You've optimised for volume, not intent. The leads are cheap because they're not actually interested in your offer; you're attracting tyre-kickers and curious browsers, not women genuinely ready to commit to a 6-week trial. Your profit has evaporated despite lower spend because you're converting almost nothing.

April's £20 profit per pound spent has become £1.90 in July. You've swapped a profitable, smaller audience for a massive, wasteful one. The algorithm is rewarding cheap clicks, not business outcomes.

**Actions**

1. **Tighten audience targeting immediately.** You've likely broadened targeting or relaxed exclusions to hit volume. Rebuild an audience around your April/May winners: women aged 25–45 in Belfast postcodes, interest in fitness/wellness, likely higher income (gym trials are discretionary spend). Exclude past non-converters and people who clicked but never progressed. Start with a tighter, smaller audience and scale slowly.

2. **Reframe creative to filter for commitment.** Your ads are probably too soft ("Join our community!"). Test messaging that emphasises the *work*—"6-week intensive trial for serious women only" or "Not for beginners: structured strength programme." You'll get fewer clicks but higher-intent leads. Lead form should ask qualifying questions (e.g., "What's your current fitness experience?" or "How committed are you to attending 3+ times weekly?"). This kills volume but saves wasted ad spend downstream.

3. **Cap daily spend at £40–50 and optimise for *purchase* not leads.** Switch your campaign objective from lead generation to conversions (trial sign-ups or sales if Meta can track them). This forces the algorithm to find people more likely to close, not just click. Your April spend of £435 generated 20 sales; you should be chasing that ROI shape, not £3 CPLs.

4. **Test a nurture sequence for warm leads.** You're losing 97% of leads. Even if targeting

fbb52f88c48e54607ffa.text:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO BRIEF

## REVENUE — ARREST THE DECLINE & LIFT YIELD PER MEMBER

• **Revenue per member is collapsing**: £138 (Apr) → £109 (Jun) is a **21% drop in 8 weeks**. Your 144 members should be generating ~£18,720/month at Feb's £130/member rate, not £15,765. **Action**: audit your pricing tier mix — are too many members on discounted intro rates? Raise intro price from day one and run a retention-focused tier upgrade campaign (email lapsed 6-month members offering class packs or premium studio time at £18–24/session).

• **June churn was clean at 3.3%, but May's 12.7% was dire**: you lost 17 members in one month after April's relocation. **Action**: post-move member survey within 14 days next time; offer 1 free class to relocated members + referral credit (£20 credit for every friend who joins by month-end). Target: hold churn under 5%.

• **Cash swing in June (−£2,521) is concerning** despite positive revenue; May's £3,633 and Feb's £3,578 show the gym can operate cash-positive. **Action**: tighten payment terms with suppliers and push GoCardless/Stripe collections to the 1st of each month (not mid-month). A 5-day cash timing fix adds £1,500+ breathing room.

---

## EXPENSES — IDENTIFY & CUT THE £20K+ MONTHLY BURN

• **March and April both show cash out >£23K** (excluding the planned April relocation capex, March's £23,328 is your baseline). Revenue in those months was £14,215 and £16,857 — meaning **operating costs are running 45–55% of turnover in normal months**. **Action**: pull your Starling statements and P&L for Mar, May, Jun in detail — split staff wages, rent, utilities, insurance, and supplier costs. Identify any recurring subscription, software, or retained contractor spend above £1,000/month that doesn't directly fill classes. Cut or renegotiate immediately. You should target **operating costs at 65% of revenue max** (leaving 35% EBITDA margin).

• **You have 144 members and (assumed) 2–3 instructors + 1 manager**: June's cash out was £20,644 on £15,765 revenue. If payroll is ~£8–10K and rent ~£3–4K, the remaining £
